AGRI History — Jan to Jul 2026
KC_01 Oil Price & Production Collapse — ANGOLA UNIQUE KILL CONDITION (weight=0.35)
Why KC_01 is Angola's Defining Risk — Oil = 90%+ of Export Earnings

Angola is sub-Saharan Africa's second-largest oil producer with 1.2M bbl/day capacity. Oil accounts for 90%+ of export earnings and approximately 60% of government revenue. The budget breakeven price is approximately $55/bbl Brent — meaning any sustained Brent move below $55 triggers immediate fiscal stress: budget deficits, inability to service Chinese debt, and cascading food import failures.

Sonangol (state oil company) production has declined from a 2008 peak of 1.8M bbl/day to ~1.1M bbl/day in 2026 — well below Angola's OPEC+ quota. The decline reflects underinvestment in upstream capacity and ageing deepwater fields. Key operators: TotalEnergies (Block 17, Block 32 — 500K bbl/day, ~15% of total Angola revenue), Chevron (Block 0 — legacy shallow-water), Eni (Angola offshore), and BP (Block 18).

The UNIQUE oil kill condition means Angola faces a dual amplification cycle: Brent decline → fiscal crisis → kwanza collapse → Chinese debt service failure → food import crisis. Each KC feeds the next in a cascade that other sub-Saharan canaries do not exhibit at this concentration.

YearProductionContext
2008 1.80M bbl/day Peak production
2010 1.75M bbl/day Post-peak plateau
2014 1.65M bbl/day Upstream challenges begin
2016 1.70M bbl/day New deepwater fields
2018 1.50M bbl/day Mature field decline
2020 1.25M bbl/day COVID demand collapse + OPEC+ cut
2022 1.15M bbl/day Structural decline; Sonangol underinvestment
2024 1.10M bbl/day Below OPEC+ quota; fiscal pressure
2026 1.10M bbl/day Current — $55/bbl breakeven barely maintained at ~$72 Brent
Q3 2026 Status: WATCH — Brent ~$72; above $55 breakeven. Sonangol production decline monitored. TotalEnergies Angola upstream delay active.

Angola Oil Economy Intelligence
TotalEnergies (TTE) — Block 17 & 32
~500K bbl/day
Largest Angola operator. ~15% of TTE global revenue from Angola. Block 17 deepwater (Dalia, Girassol). BEARISH from ops disruption.
Chevron (CVX) — Block 0
INVERSE BULLISH
Block 0 Angola operator BUT ~80%+ non-Angola revenue. Angola production collapse → oil price rises → CVX net positive via price effect.
Sonangol — State Oil Company
1.10M bbl/day
Production decline from 1.8M (2008) to 1.1M (2026). Below OPEC+ quota. Underinvestment in upstream. KC_01 primary signal driver.
Budget Breakeven
~$55/bbl
Brent above $55 = fiscal equilibrium maintained. Brent below $55 = KC_01 WATCH. Brent below $45 = KC_01 CRITICAL (0.85 score).

CVX / XOM — Inverse Bullish Transmission Logic
INVERSE BULLISH: Why Angola Production Collapse is Net Positive for CVX & XOM

Standard logic: Angola oil production collapse = Angola revenue collapse = bearish for Angola oil operators.
But CVX and XOM are different — their Angola exposure is a small fraction of global operations:

Chevron (CVX): Block 0 Angola operator, but Angola contributes roughly 5-8% of CVX total production. CVX produces heavily in the Permian, Gulf of Mexico, Kazakhstan, and Australia. When Angola (1.2M bbl/day) cuts production → global oil supply tightens → Brent rises. Higher Brent on CVX's 3M+ bbl/day non-Angola production = NET POSITIVE for CVX earnings. The Angola ops disruption is swamped by the global price lift.

ExxonMobil (XOM): Even smaller Angola stake. XOM produces ~3.7M BOE/day globally. Angola production cut → Brent price rise → XOM net benefit from price across its global portfolio.

Contrast with TotalEnergies (TTE): TTE has ~500K bbl/day in Angola (Block 17, 32) = ~15% of TTE global production. The direct Angola ops loss is large enough to overwhelm the price benefit. TTE = BEARISH. HAL/BKR (oilfield services) have zero price-side benefit — pure ops revenue loss.

Angola cut (1.2M bbl/day) → global supply -1% → Brent +$3-5/bbl
CVX non-Angola prod ~3M bbl/day × $3 price lift = +$9M/day gross benefit
CVX Angola ops loss ~60K bbl/day × $72 Brent = -$4.3M/day loss
NET: CVX positive $4.7M/day = INVERSE BULLISH
